mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover D300 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned AJ20D6 engine typically costs £5,500–£8,200 supply only, with fitting adding £1,500–£2,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Range Rover.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover D300 (AJ20D6) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the AJ20D6 is a significantly more reliable engine than the 306DT V6 it replaced. It's a modern, well-engineered inline-six with a chain-driven valvetrain and improved emissions systems. The main watchpoints are EGR cooler leaks (particularly on L405-era cars) and cooling system integrity — both are manageable with proper maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover D300 engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: EGR cooler coolant leak (more common on L405-era cars), cooling system issues (water pump, thermostat), and DPF regeneration issues from short journeys. The engine itself is fundamentally robust and not prone to the catastrophic failures seen on some earlier JLR diesels.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover D300 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. A Range Rover D300 is a £50,000–£100,000+ vehicle — a £6,500 recon is only 6–13% of the car's value, making replacement a clear decision. This is well within our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover D300 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The D300 engine code is AJ20D6 — a 3.0-litre inline-six diesel. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the EGR cooler failure on a D300?

acceptedAnswer.text: On L405-era cars, early EGR cooler replacement is the most effective prevention — many specialists recommend replacing it as a preventative measure at 60,000–80,000 miles. On L460-era cars, the design was revised, making failure less likely. If you notice coolant loss, have the EGR cooler inspected immediately.

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover D300 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

problems.title: EGR Cooler Leak
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, occasional burning smell, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,200 (EGR cooler replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before overheating)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant levels drop

problems.recommendation: The EGR cooler is a known weak point on early AJ20D6 engines (L405 era). If you notice coolant loss with no visible leak, or white smoke from the exhaust, have the EGR cooler inspected immediately. Later L460 revisions improved the design, but it remains a watchpoint. Catching it early prevents overheating damage.

problems.title: Cooling System Integrity (Water Pump & Thermostat)
problems.symptoms: Slow warm-up, temperature fluctuations, coolant loss, heater not working properly, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £400–£900 (water pump and thermostat replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.recommendation: The AJ20D6's cooling system is complex, with electric water pumps and multiple thermostats. If you notice temperature fluctuations or slow warm-up, have the system inspected. Replacing a failing water pump preventatively is far cheaper than the overheating damage it can cause.

problems.title: DPF Regeneration Issues (Short Journey Use)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced power, increased fuel consumption, automatic stop-start unavailable.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (DPF clean or forced regeneration) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless the DPF is physically damaged)
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the AJ20D6 needs regular long runs to regenerate the DPF. If your D300 is used primarily for short journeys, plan a 30–45 minute motorway run every two weeks to allow passive regeneration. If the DPF warning light comes on, book a forced regeneration promptly.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover D300 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward — catch it before it causes overheating
Periodic service — forced regeneration or clean
Viable on lower-mileage examples; assess overall engine health
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover D300? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented EGR cooler service history (or replaced on L405-era cars)
* Cooling system maintained with recent water pump and thermostat work
* Regular long runs (DPF health) — or proof of forced regenerations
* Full JLR service history with correct oil spec
avoidIf: * Coolant loss with no diagnosis on L405-era D300 — EGR cooler may be failing
* Temperature fluctuations with no cooling system history — walk away
* DPF warning light with no regeneration history — could be a sign of heavy clogging
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — potentially expensive emissions system issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJ20D6 is a fundamentally superior engine to the 306DT V6 it replaced — smoother, more powerful, and more efficient. It's not without its watchpoints — EGR cooler and cooling system integrity are the two main areas to monitor — but it's a more reliable base. Buy on full service history, and pay attention to L405-era EGR cooler records. A well-maintained D300 is a long-lived flagship diesel.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover D300 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover D300 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler leak is a known issue on L405-era cars
* Cold Start — Listen for smooth start; no chain rattle or injector noise on this engine
* Service History — including which generation (L405 or L460) and confirmed maintenance
* DPF Status — Check for warning lights; short journeys can cause clogging
* Temperature Gauge — Check for fluctuations; water pump or thermostat may be failing
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved emissions issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
